entry.title: Building a Customer Master Data Input Form Using SAC Scripting
entry.content:
Business Scenario / Problem Statement

Long-range forecasting often needs to plan for things that haven't happened yet — for example, revenue for customers a company intends to onboard in the future. Since these customers don't exist in the planning master data yet, users need a way to create them directly, along with control over attributes like their planning status.

This gave rise to two problems I needed to solve:

  1. Creating new master data on the fly: business users needed a controlled way to add a new customer to the planning model dimension and maintain attributes like Customer Status (Active/Inactive for planning) whenever required — without needing broader access to the planning model itself.
  2. Auto-incrementing the Customer ID: since customer IDs follow a simple numeric sequence, manually asking users to key in the next available ID is error-prone and invites duplicates. The form needed to work out the next ID on its own each time it loads.

With the help of SACs inbuilt scripting capabilities — it gives a limited set of business users a simple, governed way to create a new planning-only customer record with an auto-generated ID, and to update attributes on that record later.

Solution Overview

I built a two-part Story using SAC's Advanced Mode scripting capabilities:

  • Create Customer Master Data — auto-generates the next available Customer ID, captures description Customer Name, and attributes UAR Number, Customer Status, and UAR Status, and writes a new member into the Customer dimension.
  • Update Customer Master Data — lets a user pick an existing (including previously self-created) customer from a dropdown and update just the Customer Status attribute.

Both forms use the SAC Planning Model scripting APIs getMembers, getMember, createMembers, and updateMembers against the Customer dimension of the planning model, all scripted directly on the widgets within the Story canvas.

Script Variables

A handful of script variables drive the whole application:

VariableTypePurpose
allmemberPlanningModelMemberHolds the JSON payload passed to createMembers / updateMembers
customerIdFilterValueReference to the customer being worked on
NewCustomerNumberNumberNumeric form of the next available Customer ID, used for the increment logic
NewCustomerStringStringString form of NewCustomerNumber, since dimension member IDs are strings
 

Walkthrough:

OnInitialization of page event:

When the page loads, the same block of logic serves both forms:

Application.showBusyIndicator("Initializing new customer...");
var customerDim = PlanningModel_1.getMembers("Customer");

For the Update form, the dropdown is rebuilt from scratch and seeded with a placeholder instruction:

Dropdown_1.removeAllItems();
Dropdown_1.addItem("Select a Customer", "Select a Customer");
Dropdown_1.setSelectedKey("Select a Customer");

for (var i = 1; i < customerDim.length; i++) {
    Dropdown_1.addItem(customerDim[i].id, customerDim[i].description);
}

Starting the loop at index 1 skips the technical "not assigned" default member that most dimensions carry at index 0 — a small but important detail if you don't want that showing up as a selectable customer.

For the Create form, the trick is generating the next Customer ID automatically. Since new customers are always appended with an incrementing numeric ID, the last entry in the dimension is always the highest existing ID:

var LatestEntry = customerDim[customerDim.length - 1].id;
NewCustomerNumber = ConvertUtils.stringToNumber(LatestEntry) + 1;
NewCustomerString = ConvertUtils.numberToString(NewCustomerNumber);
Text_9.applyText(NewCustomerString);

The ID needs to round-trip between number and string because incrementing a string doesn't work the way you'd want ("740" + 1 isn't "741" in JavaScript), but the dimension member ID itself has to be written back as a string.

OnClick event of Button (Submit) - Creating a New Customer:

On submit of the create form, the values entered in the input fields and radio button groups are packaged into a single member object and pushed to the model:

var custName = InputField_2.getValue().trim();
var UARNo = InputField_3.getValue().trim();
var CustomerStatus = RadioButtonGroup_1.getSelectedKey();
var UARStatus = RadioButtonGroup_2.getSelectedKey();

allmember = ({
  id: NewCustomerString,
  description: custName,
  properties: { UAR_NO: UARNo, CustomerStatus: CustomerStatus, UAR_Status: UARStatus }
});

PlanningModel_1.createMembers("Customer", allmember);

Once the new member is created, the form resets itself and — this is the part I like — increments the counter again and re-populates the next available Customer ID, so the user can keep creating customers back-to-back without ever touching the ID field manually:

Dropdown_1.addItem(NewCustomerString, custName);
NewCustomerNumber = NewCustomerNumber + 1;
NewCustomerString = ConvertUtils.numberToString(NewCustomerNumber);
Text_9.applyText(NewCustomerString);

Note the dropdown on the Update form is also updated in the same step, so a customer created in this session is immediately available for editing without a page refresh.

OnSelect event of Dropdown- Selecting the customer to updates its properties:

When a user picks a customer from the dropdown on the Update form, the script reads that member's current properties and reflects them on screen — in this case, setting the Active/Inactive radio button to match the stored CustomerStatus:

var selectedId = Dropdown_1.getSelectedKey();

if (selectedId === "Select a Customer") {
    RadioButtonGroup_3.setSelectedKey("Active");
    return;
}

var customerDim = PlanningModel_1.getMember("Customer", selectedId);
RadioButtonGroup_3.setSelectedKey(customerDim.properties.CustomerStatus);

OnClick event of Button(Submit) - Updating properties of Customer:

On submit, only the changed attribute is sent back — there's no need to resend the description or other unrelated properties:

var custId = Dropdown_1.getSelectedKey();
var CustomerStatus = RadioButtonGroup_3.getSelectedKey();

if (custId === "Select a Customer") {
  Application.showMessage(ApplicationMessageType.Error, "Customer ID is required.");
  return;
}

allmember = ({
  id: custId,
  properties: { CustomerStatus: CustomerStatus }
});

PlanningModel_1.updateMembers("Customer", allmember);

This is a nice illustration of updateMembers behavior: you don't have to pass the full member object, only the ID plus the properties you actually want to change.

Conclusion

Planning cycles don't wait for master data to catch up, and this is a simple example of how a bit of scripting inside a Story can close that gap without any dependency on the source system. With just two forms and a handful of script variables, business users get a self-service way to onboard a new customer for planning purposes — with the ID handled automatically — and to keep that customer's status current as plans evolve.

entry.title: Still Using Passwords for ALM/APM API Calls? Here's When JWT Is the Better Choice
entry.content:
Should You Move from Password-Only API Calls to JWT?

A practical guide for customers integrating with SAP Agent Lifecycle Management and Agent Performance Management APIs

Part 1 of 2

If your integration currently authenticates to our REST APIs with a username and password, you are in good company. Many teams start there because it is straightforward to wire up.

This guidance applies to SAP Agent Lifecycle Management (ALM) and SAP Agent Performance Management (APM) System APIs. Both products support the same authentication model described in the ALM and APM API guides.

As usage grows, teams usually ask:

  • "Can we reduce risk if credentials are exposed?"

  • "Can we improve reliability for scheduled jobs and middleware?"

  • "Can we manage access more cleanly across multiple services?"

That is where JWT-based access becomes useful.

This post explains when JWT is a better fit, and what changes in your integration model.

What is JWT?

JWT stands for JSON Web Token. It is a compact, standardized way to represent claims (pieces of information) as a signed string that can be sent with an HTTP request.

In ALM/APM System API integrations, a JWT replaces the session id you would otherwise obtain from the login API. Instead of calling POST /REST/auth/login and passing CaldSessionId on every request, your integration signs a JWT with a private key and sends it as a Bearer token:

Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJzdWIiOi...

 

Anatomy of a JWT

A JWT has three sections separated by periods ( . )

header.payload.signature

Each section is Base64Url-encoded (not encrypted). Anyone who has the token can decode and read the header and payload. Security comes from the signature, not from hiding the contents.

SectionPurpose

Header

Metadata about the token, including the signing algorithm (for ALM/APM: typ: JWT, alg: RS256)

Payload

The claims — key/value pairs such as user id (sub), audience (aud), and expiration (exp)

Signature

Cryptographic proof that the header and payload were signed with your private key and have not been tampered with

The server validates the signature using the public key registered on your user's UsrSet record. If the signature is valid and the claims (especially exp) check out, the request is authenticated.

What JWT is — and is not

  • JWT is a signed token format for proving identity and authorization for a limited time

  • JWT is not encryption — do not put secrets in the payload

  • JWT is not a replacement for having an API user — the sub claim identifies which user you are acting as

  • JWT is not managed by the ALM/APM session layer — you generate, sign, and expire tokens on the client side

If you have only worked with username/password login so far, the mental model shift is simple: instead of asking the server for a session id, you bring your own proof of identity on each request, signed with a key the server already trusts.

How JWTs are generated

ALM/APM does not issue JWTs for you. Your integration creates and signs each token on the client side before calling the API. Common approaches:

Approach

Best for

Shell helper script

Quick testing with curl, CI smoke tests

Node.js / Python script

Scheduled jobs, middleware, reusable utilities

API client pre-request scripts

Interactive testing in Bruno or Postman

Application code

Production integrations (Java, .NET, etc.)

All approaches follow the same steps: build the header and payload claims, sign with RS256 using your private key, and pass the result as Authorization: Bearer <token>. Part 2 walks through each option with examples.

Prerequisites (both auth methods)

Regardless of whether you use User/Password or JWT authentication, the following must be true:

  • The System APIs feature must be enabled

  • A valid User Id must exist in the ALM User entity

  • That user must have access to the System APIs portal

  • The client IP address must be allowed by the system.api.ip.whitelist system option (if the option is not set, any client IP is valid)

If any of these are missing, authentication will fail before your integration logic even runs.

How ALM/APM REST authentication works today

The application supports both User/Password authentication and JWT authentication. When using JWT, you do not call the login API resource.

Pattern A: User/Password session (CaldSessionId)

  1. Call POST {{baseURL}}REST/auth/login with form fields u and p

  2. On success (200 OK), parse the response for CaldSessionId (XML: <cald-sessionid>, JSON: cald-sessionid)

  3. Send that value on subsequent API calls in the CaldSessionId header

  4. Optionally call GET {{baseURL}}REST/auth/logout when finished

Example login:

BASE_URL="https://your-tenant.example.com/PM/"

curl -sS -X POST "${BASE_URL}REST/auth/login" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  --data-urlencode "u=YOUR_API_USER" \
  --data-urlencode "p=YOUR_PASSWORD"

Important notes from the product documentation:

  • A failed login returns 403 Forbidden, not 401

  • The CaldSessionId cannot be shared across sessions from different IP addresses

  • Session duration can be configured for long-running access; there is no built-in expiry claim like JWT

Example authenticated API call:

PARAM1_VALUE="sample-value"

curl -sS -X POST "${BASE_URL}REST/query/run" \
  -H "CaldSessionId: ${CALD_SESSION_ID}" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  --data-urlencode "qbqueryid=YOUR_QUERY_ID" \
  --data-urlencode "PARAM1=${PARAM1_VALUE}"

 

Pattern B: JWT Bearer token (Authorization: Bearer ...)

  1. Register your public key on the user's UsrSet record (see Part 2)

  2. Sign a JWT locally with your private key

  3. Send API requests with Authorization: Bearer <token>

  4. Regenerate the token when exp is reached

Example API call:

BASE_URL="https://your-tenant.example.com/PM/"
JWT_TOKEN="eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCIsImtpZCI6Im...<snip>"
PARAM1_VALUE="sample-value"

curl -sS -X POST "${BASE_URL}REST/query/run" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $JWT_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  --data-urlencode "qbqueryid=YOUR_QUERY_ID" \
  --data-urlencode "PARAM1=${PARAM1_VALUE}"

Key differences at a glance:

Topic

User/Password

JWT

Login API required?

Yes

No

Auth header

CaldSessionId

Authorization: Bearer

Credential on user record

Password

Public key (PEM)

Expiry

System/session configuration

exp claim set by client

Logout API

Yes (GET /REST/auth/logout)

Not applicable

 

First, a clear framing

JWT is not a replacement for having an API user identity.
JWT is a signed token format that proves your integration is authorized to act as that user for a limited time.

JWT authentication is intended mainly for machine-to-machine communication. It is based on a private/public key pair instead of username and password. For JWT auth, there are no passwords involved in API calls.

Think of it like checking into a hotel:

  • Your API user (sub) identifies who is calling

  • Your private key proves the caller is authorized to sign on behalf of that user

  • The JWT is the room keycard (what you can access, for how long)

 

Why session/password patterns become painful over time

When integrations scale, teams often run into:

  1. Higher blast radius if passwords are leaked from scripts or CI configs

  2. Session lifecycle complexity across multiple jobs, servers, and IP addresses

  3. Harder rotation because credentials are deeply embedded in automation

  4. Operational friction when many services call the same tenant APIs

Session-based auth can still work for small, low-frequency integrations.
It becomes harder to govern as footprint and call volume increase.

What JWT changes (and why that can help)

With JWT, your system typically:

  1. Signs a short-lived token locally with your registered private key

  2. Calls APIs using Authorization: Bearer <token>

  3. Regenerates the token when exp is reached or on auth failure

Benefits for ALM/APM integrations:

  • No passwords in API traffic: authentication is signature-based

  • Client-controlled expiry: you set exp when signing the token

  • Cleaner automation: token signing fits scheduled jobs and middleware

  • Key rotation support: the system keeps a PreviousPublicKey during rotation (see Part 2)

It is not magic. It is better risk and lifecycle management.

ALM/APM JWT profile (from product documentation)

When JWT is enabled for your user, tokens must include:

Header (required unless noted):

Field

Requirement

typ

JWT (required)

alg

RS256 (required)

kid

Key id (optional)

Payload:

Claim

Requirement

Description 

sub

Required

User id; the matching PublicKey on the UsrSet record is used for validation

iss

Optional

Name or URL of the token issuer

aud

Required

Application external URL (sys.url.ext from properties)

iat

Required

Token issued-at timestamp

exp

Required

Token expiration timestamp

Important details:

  • aud must match your tenant's external URL (your baseURL without trailing slash issues — match sys.url.ext exactly)

  • JWT expiry is not maintained by the system; you decide validity when generating the token

  • While using JWT, do not pass CaldSessionId

 

When JWT is usually a better choice

JWT tends to be the better fit when you have one or more of these conditions:

  • You run scheduled jobs, middleware, or multiple services calling ALM/APM APIs

  • You want passwordless machine-to-machine integration

  • You need client-controlled, short-lived access windows

  • You want cleaner key rotation without changing application passwords

If your integration is tiny, low-frequency, and tightly controlled, session-based password auth might still be acceptable in the short term.

What JWT does not solve by itself

To keep expectations realistic:

  • JWT does not replace API user authorization rules in the tenant

  • JWT does not remove the need to protect your private key

  • JWT does not bypass IP whitelist or System APIs portal access requirements

Security comes from implementation quality, not from the token acronym.

Practical adoption path

A smooth migration usually looks like this:

  1. Generate an RSA key pair and update the public key on the user's UsrSet record (see Part 2)

  2. Start with one non-critical integration flow

  3. Add JWT signing and Bearer header usage

  4. Implement retry handling for auth failures

  5. Roll out to additional services/jobs

  6. Retire session/password usage in automation where JWT is stable

This phased approach keeps risk low and avoids "big bang" changes.

Implementation guardrails to plan now

If you adopt JWT, bake these in from day one:

  1. Use short-lived tokens (set exp conservatively)

  2. Keep private keys in a secure secret store, never in source control

  3. Match required claims exactly (sub, aud, iat, exp)

  4. Use HTTPS everywhere and never log raw tokens

  5. Plan key rotation using PublicKey and PreviousPublicKey on the user record

If these are not in scope yet, you are not blocked, but you are not done.

Coming next: hands-on examples

In Part 2, I will share step-by-step guidance for:

  • generating a public/private key pair with OpenSSL and keytool

  • updating the public key on the UsrSet record

  • multiple ways to generate and sign JWTs (shell, Node.js, Python, API clients)

  • calling protected endpoints with Bearer auth

  • rotating keys using PreviousPublicKey

If you have only used password/session auth so far, Part 2 is the shortest path to a safe JWT rollout in real systems.

entry.title: PY-GB: Mandating of Payrolling of Benefits from April 2027 – Update
entry.content:
On 15th June HMRC notified SAP that the mandating of payrolling of benefits will now be a phased roll out instead of big bang approach.

As the phased roll out includes Medical Benefits from April 2027 SAP still anticipate significant changes will be required. Currently non car benefits are processed in a very simplistic way based on the values input on IT0014 or 0015. Our understanding is that this processing will need to be extended so that annual amounts of benefit are adjusted over the remaining pay periods in the tax year to meet HMRCs requirements. The changes required to support the processing of medical benefit will then provide the framework for all subsequent non car related benefits when they too are mandated for payrolling and reporting via the FPS. 

Key changes (not an exhaustive list): 

  • Update processing for non car benefits to use annual values and remaining payroll periods in the calculation of per period adjustments. 
  • Ability to identify benefits as applicable for Class 1A NICs and map to relevant FPS data items 
  • Even though 94 RTI data items have been removed for the April 2027 scope there still needs to be 32 PBIK fields added to the RTI table and FPS report.  
  • The RTI data items for the reporting of PBIK Class 1A NICs contributions have not been removed from scope so it appears that these will  be reported through the FPS from April 2027 rather than via the P11D(b) as they are currently. This includes both mandated and voluntary payrolled benefits.  
  • The P11D process will need to be updated to remove company cars, vans and medical benefits and exclude PBIK from the Class 1A NICs for tax year 2027-28. SAP anticipate that changes will be required to the P11D form for completion by July 2028 (2027-28 tax year) 

 

SAP has reviewed the outstanding questions we have with HMRC and most remain valid and require clarification. Hopefully once the updated guidance is published by HMRC in July it will provide the clarifications required by SAP and we will be in the position to provide further updates on the planned changes required for April 2027. 

 Please also see the UKI Finance And HCM Localisation workzone page  Migration from P11D to PBIK

entry.title: TAN-Based Exemption Functionality in SAP India Localization: Complete Guide to Section 197 Lower TDS
entry.content:
Introduction.

Many times, a vendor receives a Lower Deduction Certificate (LDC) from the Income Tax Department under Section 197. In such cases, TDS should not be deducted at the normal rate. Instead, it should be deducted at the lower rate mentioned in the certificate.

The challenge comes when the certificate has a validity period, exemption percentage, and threshold limit. Manually tracking all these details for every vendor can be difficult and can easily lead to mistakes.

To handle this requirement, SAP provides a standard functionality called TAN-Based Exemption in India Localization. Using this functionality, the system can automatically apply the lower TDS rate up to the approved limit and then switch back to the normal TDS rate once the limit is exhausted.

In this blog, we will understand the complete configuration and testing of TAN-Based Exemption with a practical example.

What is Section 197 of the Indian Income Tax Act, 1961?

Imagine this: A vendor is expected to pay very little tax at the end of the financial year. In such cases, why should the buyer deduct the full TDS amount from their payments? It makes more sense to deduct only a lower or even nil amount.

So, who decides this lower amount?

This is where Section 197 comes into the picture.

Section 197 empowers the Assessing Officer (AO) to issue a certificate for a lower rate or nil deduction of TDS if the recipient’s estimated total tax liability for the financial year justifies such relief.

But why is this provision needed?

It prevents unnecessary cash flow blockages for the vendor. When the final tax liability is lower than the statutory TDS rates, deducting tax at the full rate would block the vendor’s working capital unnecessarily. Section 197 ensures a fair and practical approach for both parties.

How to Identify Vendors with AO Exemption?

When the Assessing Officer approves the request, they issue a Lower Deduction Certificate to the vendor. This certificate contains important details such as:

  • The allowed lower TDS rate
  • The validity period
  • The threshold limit (if any)

Which Sections Allow Lower/Nil Deduction Certificates?

Lower Deduction Certificates under Section 197 can be issued for several TDS sections, including:

  • 194C (Contractors)
  • 194H (Commission)
  • 194I (Rent)
  • 194J (Professional/Technical Fees)
  • And many others.

Now Coming to SAP

How to handle Lower Deduction Certificates in Withholding Tax in SAP?

Let’s understand this with a practical example:

  • Vendor Code: 500 (DS Company)
  • Normal TDS Rate: 2% (Withholding Tax Code: S8)
  • Lower TDS Rate: 0.25% (87.50% Exemption)
  • Value Limit: ₹5,00,000
  • Certificate Validity: 25/05/2026 to 31/07/2026

What does this mean?

Up to ₹5,00,000 of cumulative invoice value, the system should deduct TDS at 0.25%. Once the vendor’s invoices cross this limit, the TDS rate should automatically revert to the normal 2%.

To achieve this intelligent behavior in standard SAP, we use the powerful TAN-Based Exemption Functionality for India Localization.

Note: This document assumes that the user is already familiar with basic withholding tax configuration for India.

 

Configuration Steps for TAN-Based Exemption Functionality

Now that we understand the concept, let’s dive into the actual SAP configuration. Follow these steps carefully to enable TAN-Based Exemption in India Localization.

1. Withholding Tax Type – Accumulation Setting

Path: Financial Accounting → Financial Accounting Global Settings → Withholding Tax → Extended Withholding Tax → Calculation → Withholding Tax Type → Define Withholding Tax Type for Invoice Posting / Payment Posting

We need to make an important change here:

  • Set the Accumulation field to ‘No Accumulation’.

     


    TAN-Based Exemption works on cumulative invoice value logic. Keeping accumulation as “No Accumulation” allows the system to correctly track the threshold limit (e.g., ₹5,00,000) without interfering with standard accumulation behavior.

2. Withholding Tax Code

  • Path: Financial Accounting → Financial Accounting Global Settings → Withholding Tax → Extended Withholding Tax → Calculation → Withholding Tax Code→ Define Withholding Tax Codes

    Ensure the checkbox “Calculation of withholding tax according to formula” is unchecked.

     

The TAN-Based Exemption functionality does not support formula-based calculation. This box must always remain unchecked; otherwise, the exemption logic will not work as expected.

3. Mapping of Invoice and Payment Tax Types

This is a critical step for seamless integration between invoice posting and payment.

Two ways to reach the mapping table:

  • Option 1 (Direct): Use Transaction SM30 → Table View: V_FIWTIN_TDS_MAP
  • Option 2 (IMG Path): Financial Accounting → Financial Accounting Global Settings → Withholding Tax → Extended Withholding Tax → Calculation → India → Exemptions and Reductions → TAN-Based Exemption → Map Invoice Time and Payment Time Tax Types and Tax Codes

 

Note: While trying to perform the mapping process, if you face a dump related to Runtime error PERFORM_NOT_FOUND which is caused due to incorrect form routine CHECK_VIEW_ENTRIES, then refer and follow the steps provided in KBA 2113231.

4. Section Code Group

Refer to the Official SAP PDF document (Link) that explains this functionality in detail. It gives clear guidance on how to set up and use Section Code Group for TAN-Based Exemption.

 

5. Business Partner (BP) Master Settings

Go to Transaction BP (Business Partner).

  • Select the vendor and open it in “Display in BP Role”.
  • Navigate to Company Code data.

 

 

Then go to the tab: Supplier: Withholding Tax

 

In this tab, do not maintain (keep it blank) the following fields:

  • W/tax ID
  • Exemption Number
  • Exem. %
  • Exempt From
  • Exempt To
  • Exemption Reason
  • Exemption Reason Description

Important: The real configuration for TAN-Based Exemption is done in the tab: Vendor: Country-Spec. Enh.

 

Maintain the required details here as per your business requirement (Lower Rate, Threshold Limit, Validity Period, etc.).

 

Final Note

Your configuration is now complete!

Key Point to Remember: This functionality will only work if you use the same Section Code during invoice posting that you have maintained in the Business Partner master.

Testing the TAN-Based Exemption in Action

Let’s go back to our example and post three sample invoices using FB60 to see how the system intelligently applies the TDS rates.

Vendor: DS Company (Vendor Code 500)

Lower Rate: 0.25% (up to ₹5,00,000)

Normal Rate: 2% (after threshold)

1. First Invoice  ₹50,000

 

As you can see, TDS is calculated at 0.25% = ₹125.

The exemption is working perfectly.

2. Second Invoice  ₹4,00,000

 

TDS is again calculated at 0.25% = ₹1,000.

Cumulative invoice value now = ₹4,50,000 (still within the ₹5,00,000 limit).

3. Third Invoice ₹2,00,000

 

This is where it gets interesting!

Here is the clear breakdown of how the system handled this invoice:

The system automatically split the invoice: it applied the lower rate (0.25%) for the remaining eligible amount within the threshold and switched to the normal 2% rate for the amount exceeding ₹5,00,000.

Result: Once the ₹5,00,000 threshold is crossed, all future invoices for this vendor will be deducted at the normal 2% rate until a new Lower Deduction Certificate is uploaded.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

While configuring TAN-Based Exemption, please keep the following points in mind:

  • Do not select the "Calculation of withholding tax according to formula" checkbox in the withholding tax code.
  • Make sure the same Section Code is maintained in the Business Partner master and used during invoice posting.
  • Do not maintain exemption details in the standard "Supplier: Withholding Tax" tab. Maintain them in the "Vendor: Country-Specific Enhancement" tab.
  • Verify the validity period, exemption percentage, threshold limit, and certificate number before testing.
  • If you face the runtime error PERFORM_NOT_FOUND while maintaining the mapping table, refer to SAP KBA 2113231.

A Few Things You'll Likely Run Into

Once this is live, you will probably still hit a couple of situations the standard help files never mention. I ran into most of these while setting up TAN-Based Exemption for a client in the FMCG space, and each one taught me something the initial testing did not show.

Certificate expires in the middle of an invoice run

This happens more often than you would expect, mainly with vendors who renew their certificate late. If the invoice date falls after the “Exempt To” date maintained on the BP master, the system does not throw any error, it simply stops applying the exemption and falls back to the normal rate. So if a vendor who was earlier getting deducted at 0.25% suddenly starts showing 2%, and nobody has changed the configuration, the first place to check is the validity period on the Vendor: Country-Spec. Enh. tab.

Advance payments and partial exemptions

This one confused our AP team for a while. Since down payments and invoices are tracked against the same TAN, the accumulated value considers both together. So if a large advance is paid to the vendor before the invoice is posted, it can quietly use up part of the threshold, and the vendor ends up crossing over to the normal rate sooner than expected. It is not a configuration error, it is the system working exactly as designed, but if the business team is not told about this beforehand, it looks like something has gone wrong.

When the threshold behaves oddly

If the accumulation is not splitting the way you expect, do not go straight to the BP master. In most cases the issue traces back to Step 1, someone has reset the Accumulation field on the withholding tax type from “No Accumulation” back to a period basis such as monthly or annual. There is no hard error for this. The system simply calculates the cumulative value on a different basis than what TAN-Based Exemption expects, and the split you saw in our third invoice example above will not happen correctly.

One habit that has saved me a fair amount of rework: always test with at least three invoices, not two. Two invoices only confirm that the lower rate is applying. It is that third invoice, the one that crosses the threshold, where the real configuration issues tend to show up.

Conclusion

That’s it!

We have successfully configured and tested the TAN-Based Exemption functionality in SAP.

As we saw in the example, the system automatically applied the lower TDS rate while the vendor was within the approved threshold limit. Once the threshold was crossed, the system automatically switched back to the normal TDS rate without any manual intervention.

This functionality helps reduce manual effort, avoids incorrect TDS calculation, and ensures that Lower Deduction Certificates are handled correctly in the system.

I hope this blog helps you understand and implement TAN-Based Exemption easily.

If you have faced any different scenarios or have any questions regarding this functionality, feel free to share them in the comments section.

Thank You,

Sudeep Suhas Durgule.

entry.title: Archive Business Process Home Page Cards with BPE Errors
entry.content:
Hello again, community!

I have only asked questions before, but today I feel like this topic, regarding a situation that happened to me while trying to clear stale BPE errors, deserves to be my very first post in the SAP Community.

As we all know, SAP is introducing for SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding, starting with the 1H2026 release, business process tasks that appear in For You Today category within the Needs Attention section on the latest Home Page, instead of appearing only in the to-do panel. But what happens when there are stale BPE errors that need to be cleaned up?

Well, before the 1H2026 release, the answer would have been to simply go to the Check Tool and run the ONB2BPEStaleTodoCleanUp check. In fact, that's exactly what I did before considering the new Home Page Business Cards. By running this check, the tasks disappeared from both the to-do panel and BPE: Manage User Tasks. So, what's the problem with that, you might be wondering? Well, when I noticed that the BPE error cards were still showing up on the latest Home Page, I realized that this check doesn't take the new Home Page cards into account. It only clears the BPE errors in the to-do panel.

So, this post is about that.

With the help of the SAP Support team, a workaround was found. It basically consists of looking up the bpUserTaskId, usually found in the URL when opening the BPE error. Then, go to the BPE: Manage User Tasks tool in Admin Center, filter using the copied User Task ID, select the result, and complete the task from within that tool, this way, the cards on the latest home page can be actually cleared. 

This process needs to be followed in that particular order. Running the ONB2BPEStaleTodoCleanUp check in the Check Tool first makes the processes disappear from the BPE: Manage User Tasks tool, making them unavailable to be archived afterward.

So, that's it! Let me know your thoughts on this workaround, and let's hope SAP comes up with an official solution eventually.

Regards from Mexico! 🎡

entry.title: How to detect inactive DTPs in SAP BW Process Chains
entry.content:
Introduction

One of the common causes of SAP BW process chain failures is the presence of inactive objects. This situation typically occurs when an object is modified during development or when dependent objects become inactive after changes are transported or activated.

Among the different BW object types, Data Transfer Processes (DTPs) are particularly critical because they are widely used in process chains. In addition, a DTP depends on other BW objects such as transformations and DataStore Objects (DSOs). Therefore, a change in one of these objects can indirectly cause a DTP to become inactive and lead to process chain failures.

The initial idea for this solution came from the following blog:

Tool to check status (active/inactive) of process chain objects, Part 1

Building on that concept, I developed an enhanced version on identifying inactive DTPs within a process chain.

 

 

Solution

To address this issue, I developed an ABAP report called ZINACTIVE_DTP.

 

How the Program Works

The report performs the following steps:

  1. Retrieves all inactive DTPs in the system and stores them in the internal table IT_INADTP.
  2. Flattens the selected process chain, including all nested process chains, and stores the resulting nodes in IT_PC_NODES_ALL.
  3. Removes nodes that are marked as skipped in the process chain definition.
  4. Compares the inactive DTP list with the process chain nodes and keeps only the DTPs that are both inactive and used in the selected process chain.
  5. Displays the final result in an ALV report.

 

Selection Screen

Enter the technical name of the process chain to be analyzed:

 

Result

After execution, the report displays all inactive DTPs that are used within the selected process chain:

 

Source Code

REPORT ZINACTIVE_DTP.


PARAMETERS: P_PC TYPE RSPC_CHAIN. "Process Chain


DATA: WA_PC_NODES TYPE RSPCCHAIN,
      IT_PC_NODES TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF RSPCCHAIN,
      IT_PC_NODES_ALL TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF RSPCCHAIN,
      IT_ALV TYPE REF TO CL_SALV_TABLE.


*****Tracking inactive DTPs
SELECT RSBKDTP~DTP,
       SRC && ' -> ' && TGT AS DTPTEXT
INTO TABLE @DATA(IT_INADTP)
FROM RSBKDTP
INNER JOIN RSBKDTPSTAT ON RSBKDTP~DTP = RSBKDTPSTAT~DTP
WHERE RSBKDTP~OBJVERS = 'A'
  AND RSBKDTPSTAT~OBJSTAT = 'INA'
.


*****Flattening process chain and removing its skipped nodes
PERFORM GET_PC_NODES USING P_PC.
IF SY-SUBRC = 0.
   APPEND LINES OF IT_PC_NODES TO IT_PC_NODES_ALL.
ENDIF.

CLEAR: WA_PC_NODES.
LOOP AT IT_PC_NODES_ALL INTO WA_PC_NODES.
     DATA(LV_TABIX) = SY-TABIX.

     "Check if the node is skipped
     SELECT SINGLE *
     INTO @DATA(WA_SKIPPED)
     FROM RSPCCHAIN_SKIP
     WHERE CHAIN_ID = @wa_PC_NODES-CHAIN_ID
       AND OBJVERS = @wa_PC_NODES-OBJVERS
       AND TYPE = @wa_PC_NODES-TYPE
       AND VARIANTE = @wa_PC_NODES-VARIANTE
       AND LNR = @wa_PC_NODES-LNR
       AND TO_BE_SKIPPED = 'X'.

     IF SY-SUBRC = 0.
        DELETE IT_PC_NODES_ALL INDEX LV_TABIX.
        CONTINUE.
     ENDIF.

     "Check if the node is of type CHAIN and expand it
     IF WA_PC_NODES-TYPE = 'CHAIN'.
        DATA(LV_LOC_PC) = CONV RSPC_CHAIN( WA_PC_NODES-VARIANTE ).
        PERFORM GET_PC_NODES USING LV_LOC_PC.
        IF SY-SUBRC = 0.
           APPEND LINES OF IT_PC_NODES TO IT_PC_NODES_ALL.
        ENDIF.
     ENDIF.
ENDLOOP.


*****Check for inactive DTPs in the selected process chain
CLEAR: WA_PC_NODES.
DELETE IT_PC_NODES_ALL WHERE TYPE NE 'DTP_LOAD'.

LOOP AT IT_INADTP INTO DATA(WA_INACTP).
     DATA(LV_TABIX1) = SY-TABIX.
     READ TABLE IT_PC_NODES_ALL
          TRANSPORTING NO FIELDS
          WITH KEY TYPE = 'DTP_LOAD' VARIANTE = WA_INACTP-DTP.

     IF SY-SUBRC <> 0.
        DELETE IT_INADTP INDEX LV_TABIX1.
     ENDIF.
ENDLOOP.


*****Display the final list based on IT_INADTP
CALL METHOD CL_SALV_TABLE=>FACTORY
     IMPORTING
         R_SALV_TABLE = IT_ALV
     CHANGING
         T_TABLE = IT_INADTP.

CALL METHOD IT_ALV->DISPLAY.



*****FORM
FORM GET_PC_NODES USING PROCESS_CHAIN TYPE RSPC_CHAIN.
CLEAR: IT_PC_NODES.
SELECT *
INTO TABLE @IT_PC_NODES
FROM RSPCCHAIN
WHERE CHAIN_ID = @Process_CHAIN
  AND OBJVERS = 'A'
.
ENDFORM.

 

 

Conclusion

The ZINACTIVE_DTP report helps BW administrators and developers proactively identify inactive DTPs before scheduled process chains are executed.

By detecting these issues in advance, you can reduce unexpected process chain failures and improve the reliability of your BW data loading processes.

I hope you find this utility useful. Feel free to share your feedback, suggestions, or possible enhancements in the comments section.

Best regards,

Hadi Jafari

entry.title: SAP pro-rata input tax in Hungary: implementation and accurate M-sheet reporting
entry.content:
Hello Community,

from 1st June 2026, Hungarian VAT Return requires more detailed VAT data on M-sheets about the pro-rata input tax related to domestic supplier invoices. In this blogpost I would like to share how the pro-rata input tax codes need to be set in your system to get an accurate data on M-sheets.

Why Hungarian VAT reporting needs special attention

In SAP Document and Reporting Compliance for Hungary, the setup must support both the deductible tax amount reported in the VAT return and the transaction-level selection logic behind the domestic purchase invoice details. This is particularly important when invoices are posted with pro-rata input tax codes, because the document remains relevant for declaration and control-list purposes even though only part of the VAT is deductible.

For Sheet 01-02, tax box 68 must capture the deductible portion of input tax. This means the condition type representing deductible input VAT must be assigned correctly in the tax box mapping so that the VAT return reflects the recoverable amount rather than the full input VAT amount.

Beyond the main VAT return, companies should also assess the impact on Sheets 07/08 and the M-sheets. This is where reporting design becomes critical: invoices posted with pro-rata tax codes must remain selectable and be handled in a way that reflects the deductible VAT logic applied in accounting.

In other words, the accounting configuration and the Hungarian statutory reporting configuration cannot be designed separately. If the tax procedure splits deductible and non-deductible VAT correctly but reporting does not recognize those invoices, the result will be incomplete or misleading declaration data.

From 1 July 2026, Hungarian M-sheet reporting becomes more deduction-oriented, requiring businesses to report not only VAT shown on supplier invoices but also the VAT actually deducted, broken down by VAT rate. This makes pro-rata handling even more important for SAP customers operating in Hungary.

Two implementation approaches for pro-rata input tax

There are generally two practical ways to support pro-rata input tax in a Hungarian company code. The first is a simpler, tax-code-based approach. The second is the standard SAP pro-rata solution, which offers a more structured and scalable design for organizations seeking a maintainable long-term model. SAP Help Portal describes the standard VAT pro-rata adjustment concept, including coefficient management, invoice application, and subsequent adjustment logic.

What needs to be in place before you start

Before implementation begins, confirm the legal and process design: which company codes apply pro-rata, which coefficient should be used during the year, how frequently it may change, and how the deductible and non-deductible portions should appear in VAT reporting. FI tax configuration owners and the Hungarian reporting team should also be aligned, as both configuration streams need to support the same reporting outcome.

Approach 1: a dedicated pro-rata tax code setup

In the simpler approach, dedicated tax codes are created for proportional input tax. This option works well when the business prefers a direct and transparent setup without introducing the full standard pro-rata framework.

The tax rate is split in line with the deduction coefficient valid for the relevant period. For example, if input VAT is 88% deductible, then 88% of the 27% VAT is treated as deductible and 12% as non-deductible. This provides a clear accounting and reporting basis for separating the two portions.

The pro-rata tax code can then be maintained in table IDHU_VATPROP but the deduction rate set in this table (field “PROPORTION”) is not considered, only the tax code and the company code. The deduction rate is derived from the values in the BSET table (based on the tax code settings).

This option is often attractive when the annual coefficient is stable, and the company prefers a straightforward operational setup.

However, if your organization requires a more sophisticated and extensible design, whereas the coefficient is changing from year to year, SAP’s pro-rata framework is usually the stronger option.

Approach 2: the standard SAP pro-rata solution

The standard SAP solution is typically the stronger choice when you need coefficient-based calculation, the ability to support future adjustments, and a design aligned with broader VAT governance. The standard pro-rata framework enables a more sophisticated coefficient[1] handling. Two different coefficients are considered one during the year (or other reporting period) and the final one at the year end. The first one is applied during invoice posting, and the second one in the subsequent adjustment postings. Please note that this article is not intended to describe the VAT Pro-Rata Adjustment process.

Reference to related Help Portal sections and SAP Notes:

Help Portal:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/bb30b322546c446ab74a1ba659cecdf2/118a5e8e82974314bca3c688f5932bf9.html?state=DRAFT&version=2023.latest

SAP Notes:

https://me.sap.com/notes/801318

https://me.sap.com/notes/867942

https://me.sap.com/notes/1126221

 

Key SAP configuration steps for year-in-progress pro-rata handling

To ensure that pro-rata tax codes work correctly with the Hungarian TAXHU procedure, the configuration should be built in a clear sequence. The objective is to calculate the deductible and non-deductible split during posting and then carry the tax base, the deductible and the non-deductible result into statutory reporting.

  1. Create the calculation formulas in the tax procedure

Create formulas 907 and 935 in accordance with points 1 and 2 of SAP Note 867942 - VAT Pro-Rata: Calculation Formula. Please note that the attachment to the note contains multiple variants for formula 935; these should be reviewed carefully, and the version relevant to the specific system should be implemented. We applied Option C. Be sure to activate the formulas after creation.

  1. Create a condition table so the deduction ratio can be maintained by company code and tax code.

We created condition table 986 based on the attachment to SAP Note 1126221 - Pro-Rata: legal change for TVA France (ProrataCustomizin.zip), section Pro-Rata enhancement using the tax code. As this is a client-independent setting, a workbench request is required. Several warning messages were displayed during the process, but these can be acknowledged and by-passed.

"Create new condition table based on condition table 85 plus field MWSKZ (Tax Code) in IMG: Sales and Distribution->Basic Functions->Pricing->Pricing Control->Define Condition Tables: Create condition tables"

Based on this condition table 986, create the access sequence that will later be used in the TAXHU procedure as a statistical line for the pro-rata calculation.

  1. Create a new access sequence (e.g. ZPR2) using condition table 986 so that the deduction ratio can be maintained in conditions by company code and tax code. This access sequence ZPR2 will later be used in the new statistical line assigned to the tax procedure TAXHU. This is a client-independent setting.

"Create new Access Sequence for new condition table in IMG: Financial Accounting->Financial Accounting Global Settings->Tax on Sales/Purchases->Basic Settings->Check Calculation Procedure: Access Sequences."

Enter the ID ZPR2.

Then add condition table 986.

 

Acknowledge this warning message to continue. A second warning message will appear and must also be confirmed. Once both messages have been acknowledged, the following screen is displayed.

Save your changes.

  1. Create a new condition type (e.g. ZPR2) in the tax procedure using the access sequence created earlier. This is a client-dependent setting.

"Create new Access Sequence for new condition table in IMG: Financial Accounting->Financial Accounting Global Settings->Tax on Sales/Purchases->Basic Settings->Check Calculation Procedure: Define Condition Types."

This condition type was also assigned the identifier ZPR2.

  1. Insert condition type ZPR2 into the TAXHU tax procedure and assign the formulas created earlier (907 and 935) to the lines where pro-rata input tax calculation is required.

(Help Portal example: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/8e9cf99e948c469eb1e7c9a582e3b0c0/0d7d810a03a9451fb8aa34c334fe5ce1.html — just replace ZRAT with ZPR2.)

At this stage, the tax procedure TAXHU is ready to calculate pro-rata input VAT.

  1. Create a pro-rata input tax code using the TAXHU procedure.

We created tax code Z5. The non-deductible portion is posted using condition type NAV, although NVV can also be used. Enter 0% on this line, as the deduction ratio will later be derived automatically based on the company code, tax code, and validity period maintained in FV11.

Don’t forget to set up account determination for the tax code. This is required for condition types VST and NAV.

Extend the tax box mapping to include the new tax code.

Assign tax code Z5 to line 68.

The requirement is to report the full tax base together with the deductible portion of input VAT. For this reason, NAV or NVV is not assigned here just VST.

  1. Maintain the year-in-progress deduction ratio in FV11 for condition type ZPR2.

Pay close attention to the validity date range.

 

Why pro-rata invoices must also be selected for Hungarian M-sheets

If an incoming invoice is posted with a pro-rata input tax code, it should be included in Hungarian domestic purchases invoice list. The invoice remains relevant for control and reconciliation purposes, and the reporting logic should capture the deductible VAT treatment consistently with the posting logic. This becomes even more important as Hungarian M-sheet reporting moves toward mandatory disclosure of VAT actually deducted by rate, including partial deduction scenarios from 1 July 2026. SAP customers should therefore ensure that invoice selection, tax box mapping, analytical views and XML output are tested together for pro-rata scenarios.

The practical takeaway is straightforward: in Hungary, pro-rata implementation is not only an FI tax configuration topic, but also a statutory reporting topic. A successful design ensures that the tax procedure calculates the deductible amount correctly, tax box 68 captures the deductible portion where relevant, and incoming invoices posted with pro-rata tax codes are properly selected and handled in the M-sheets.

-------------------------------------------------------------------

[1] The coefficients must be calculated outside of the system according the Act on VAT.

entry.title: Blog#8: How Custom Mail Notifications are used and configured in SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
In my last post, I shared how custom consistency checks make sure a ticket is complete before it moves forward in SAP Cloud ALM. This time it is about what happens right after that moment. Once a ticket moves forward, the right people should know about it. With pony, custom mail notifications can be configured centrally, so they are sent automatically to the right partner.

SAP Cloud ALM already comes with many standard notifications, and each user can activate them individually for their own work. In practice, a process often needs one step more: a rule that a specific partner is always informed when a ticket reaches a specific point, independent of personal settings. A custom mail notification turns exactly that into central configuration, defined once and valid for the whole team.


Let's be specific: The developer is notified when implementation starts

Here is a concrete example from one of my projects. On a Feature, the "Additional Information" section holds the partner functions, in our example the "Responsible" and the "Developer". When the Feature moves to the status "In Implementation", an email is sent automatically to the assigned developer.

The mail informs the developer that there is a Feature to be implemented and that she is the assigned developer. It contains a few details about the Feature and a link that leads directly into the ticket, so the work can start right away. Nobody has to remember to notify the developer, the notification follows from the process itself.

Mail example

 

More than one recipient: notifications that follow your partner functions

The example above notifies the developer, but the same mechanism covers many situations:

  • Recipients are chosen from the partner functions of the ticket: the "Responsible", "Tester", the current user, and all other partner functions available on the artifact
  • The content comes from a mail template with its own subject and text
  • Placeholders bring the details of the ticket into the mail, and all fields available on the artifact can be used, including the custom fields created with pony
  • The trigger can be tied to a status change, so the notification is sent exactly at the point in the process where it matters

This way, the notification concept from your process design becomes part of SAP Cloud ALM itself: the right people are informed automatically when a ticket needs their attention.


How it is configured, without code

Custom mail notifications are set up in a graphical editor. No developer required.

In the pony administration, we find two apps that work together: the "Mail Templates" and the "Workflows".

A mail template defines what the email contains: a template name, the artifact type, the subject and the text. The right-hand column of the editor lists all the fields available on the artifact as placeholders, including the custom fields, so the mail can carry exactly the details the recipient needs.

Mail editor 

The sending itself is a workflow, in our example "Feature Notification", built in the same three logical steps as the consistency checks from my last post:

  • Step 1 - When the notification runs: the trigger, in our example the status change of a Feature
  • Step 2 - What is verified: the check, in our example whether the Feature is in the status "In Implementation"
  • Step 3 - What happens: the action "Send Email", with the recipient taken from the partner functions (in our example the "Developer") and the mail template to be sent

Details of the Workflow

 

Once the workflow is published, it is active immediately. From that moment, the developer is informed automatically whenever a Feature moves into implementation.

In summary: custom mail notifications bring the notification rules of your process directly into SAP Cloud ALM. Configured once, centrally, they make sure the right people are informed automatically at the right moment, with the details they need and a direct link into the ticket. Notifications are no longer a question of personal settings. They are part of the process itself.

In my next post, I will share how text templates can be used for tickets in SAP Cloud ALM, so recurring content is written once and reused consistently.

entry.title: NR4 Canada Report in SAP DRC – Available for On-Premise and Private Edition, soon in Public Edition
entry.content:
Introduction

Canadian tax compliance just got a significant upgrade in SAP. We are excited to announce that the NR4 withholding tax report for Canada is now available in SAP S/4HANA 2023 and SAP S/4HANA 2025, released via SAP Note 3748353. In addition, it is planned to be delivered in SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS02 (October 2026) and in SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2608.2 (October 2026).

The report is delivered as part of SAP Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC) — the same solution that already hosts the T4A-NR report — giving Canadian customers a consistent, unified experience for non-resident withholding tax reporting.

This new report directly addresses a long-standing need expressed by our Canadian customers and complements the existing T4A-NR report that was already available in SAP.

What Is the NR4 Report?

The NR4 – Statement of Amounts Paid or Credited to Non-Residents of Canada is a form mandated by the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA). It must be filed by Canadian payers who have paid or credited amounts — such as dividends, royalties, rental income, management fees, or other passive income — to non-residents of Canada during a calendar year.

For every non-resident recipient, a corresponding NR4 slip must be issued, and an NR4 summary must be submitted to the CRA. This is a critical reporting obligation under the Income Tax Act of Canada and is distinct from the T4A-NR, which covers payments to non-residents for services rendered in Canada (such as acting or performance fees).

What's New: NR4 Report in SAP S/4HANA & SAP S/4HANA Cloud

With the delivery of this new functionality, SAP S/4HANA & SAP S/4HANA Cloud now supports the automated generation of NR4 slips and summary directly from the withholding tax postings recorded in the system.

Key capabilities of the new NR4 report include:

  • Extraction of withholding tax data posted against non-resident vendors and business partners
  • Income type classification according to CRA-defined NR4 income codes (e.g., dividends, royalties, rents, management fees)
  • Currency conversion handling for amounts paid in foreign currencies, in line with CRA requirements
  • Country of residence derivation from business partner master data
  • Tax treaty code mapping based on the non-resident's country of tax residency
  • Gross income and withholding tax amounts per recipient, per income type
  • Output in the format required for CRA electronic filing

How It Complements the T4A-NR

We have heard your feedback. Many of our Canadian customers highlighted that while the T4A-NR report covered payments to non-residents for services, there was no equivalent SAP-delivered solution for the NR4, which covers passive income payments to non-residents. The NR4 report fills this gap.

 

Feature

T4A-NR

NR4

Payment type

Services rendered in Canada

Passive income (dividends, royalties, rents, etc.)

Recipient

Non-residents providing services

Non-residents receiving passive income

CRA form

T4A-NR slip

NR4 slip

Available in SAP DRC

Yes

Now available

 

Configuration: What You Need to Set Up

At the Business Partner Level

A key prerequisite is the correct setup of the Business Partner master data. For the NR4 report to correctly identify and extract non-resident payees, the following must be maintained at the business partner level:

  • Country of residence / tax country: The country where the non-resident is tax-resident must be maintained. This drives both the reporting and the applicable treaty withholding tax rate.
  • Withholding tax data: The relevant withholding tax type and code for Canada NR4 must be assigned to the business partner, including the non-resident's tax identification number (e.g., the Social Insurance Number, Business Number, or foreign TIN as applicable).
  • NR4 income type code: The CRA income type code (e.g., code 09 for rental income, code 11 for royalties, code 13 for management fees, code 25 for dividends) must be configured and associated with the applicable withholding tax type/code combination.

For full configuration details at the business partner level, refer to the SAP Help Portal: Business Partner Configuration for NR4 – SAP Help Portal

In SAP Document and Reporting Compliance

The NR4 report is executed from within SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, the same way you already run the T4A-NR report. It allows you to:

  1. Select the reporting year and company code(s)
  2. Filter by withholding tax type relevant to NR4
  3. Generate the output for review, correction, and submission to the CRA

For the full functional documentation of the report, refer to the SAP Help Portal: NR4 Withholding Tax Report for Canada – SAP Help Portal

 

Availability Summary

 

EditionReleaseAvailability
SAP S/4HANA On-Premise & SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition2023Available – SAP Note 3748353
SAP S/4HANA On-Premise & SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition2025Available – SAP Note 3748353
SAP S/4HANA On-Premise & SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition2025 FPS02Planned – October 2026
SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition2608.2Planned – October 2026 

 

Getting Started

If you are on SAP S/4HANA 2023 or 2025 on-premise, implement SAP Note 3748353 to activate the NR4 report. Ensure your Basis team has applied the note and that your Finance/Tax team reviews the business partner configuration prerequisites before the next reporting cycle.

If you are on SAP S/4HANA Cloud, the functionality will be available with the 2608.2 release in October 2026 — no manual note implementation required.

 

We Want Your Feedback

The NR4 report was developed in direct response to customer demand, and your input continues to shape what we build next. If you have questions, configuration challenges, or suggestions for future enhancements, we invite you to share your feedback through our dedicated Work Zone community:

Canada ERP Localization – Work Zone

We look forward to hearing from you.

entry.title: Media Handling in SAP CAP: Mixing Static and Uploaded Images in Fiori Elements V4
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

Introduction

When building applications with the SAP Cloud Application Programming (CAP) model and SAP Fiori Elements V4, handling media files is a common requirement. Usually, it is straightforward: first define a LargeBinary field, and the framework generates an upload control.

However, things get complicated when we need a single table column to dynamically display images from two different sources:

  1. Static default images (loaded via CSV during deployment).

  2. Custom images uploaded by users via the UI.

This guide covers the complete end-to-end setup to achieve this:

  • Data Modeling — Defining the schema with a "Traffic Cop" virtual field.

  • UI Annotations — Exposing the right fields to the List Report and Object Page.

  • Backend Logic — Intercepting OData V4 requests in Node.js to bypass UI optimizations.

  • HANA Deployment — Resolving common driver version conflicts during deployment.

Why I Wrote This

Recently, I was developing an application on the SAP Business Technology Platform where users needed to browse a catalog of items. Some items had static placeholder images mapped to local CSV paths, while others had high-res photos explicitly uploaded by users.

When I attempted to route these dynamically using a virtual field, my Fiori V4 List Report stubbornly displayed blank "No Image" placeholders. After hours of debugging the OData V4 network payload, I realized Fiori was optimizing my request by stripping out the hidden columns my backend logic relied on. I couldn't find a definitive guide covering this specific Fiori V4 optimization trap, so I'm sharing the bulletproof workaround I built.

 

First, Let's Understand the Fiori V4 Optimization Trap

Before looking at the code, it helps to understand why the standard approach fails in Fiori Elements V4.

Fiori V4 is aggressively optimized for bandwidth. When it loads a List Report, the OData $select query only asks for the columns explicitly visible on the screen. If the backend relies on a virtual field (e.g., displayImageUrl) that calculates its value based on hidden fields (like mediaType or ImageUrl), those hidden fields arrive at the backend handler as undefined. We cannot calculate a dynamic URL if the framework refuses to fetch the underlying data.

To fix this, we don't fight the UI. Instead, we let Fiori request whatever it wants, and we use a custom CAP backend hook to silently query the database for the missing fields before delivering the final payload to the browser.

 

Architecture Overview

This solution relies on a clean separation between the database schema, the UI annotations, and the backend Node.js service.

project-root/
│
├── db/
│   ├── data/
│   │   └── mediahandling.db-Image.csv  ← Initial data linking to static image paths
│   └── schema.cds                      ← Defines the binary, static, and virtual fields
│
├── app/
│   ├── mediahandling/                  ← Your Fiori Elements application
│   │   └── webapp/
│   │       └── images/                 
│   │           ├── image1.jpg          ← The actual static placeholder files
│   │           └── image2.jpg
│   └── annotations.cds                 ← Maps the UI controls for Fiori Elements
│
└── srv/
    ├── catalogService.cds              ← Exposes the OData service
    └── catalogService.js               ← Contains the custom 'READ' interceptor logic

 

Step 1: The Data Model (schema.cds)

We need an entity that stores both the binary file and the static URL, alongside a virtual field to decide which one to render.

Critical Detail: Ensure the @ui.IsImageURL: true annotation is applied directly to the virtual field here in the schema. This instructs Fiori V4 to natively allocate an avatar control in the List Report.

namespace mediahandling.db;
using { cuid, managed } from '@sap/cds/common';

entity Image: cuid, managed {
    Name        : String(100);
    Description : String(255);
    
    // Holds the static CSV path (e.g., 'images/default.jpg')
    ImageUrl    : String; 

    // The actual uploaded file data
    @Core.MediaType  : mediaType
    image       : LargeBinary;

    // The file extension/type (e.g., 'image/jpeg')
    @Core.IsMediaType: true
    mediaType   : String;

    // The "Traffic Cop" virtual field for Fiori UI display
    @ui.IsImageURL: true
    virtual displayImageUrl : String;
}

 

Step 2: Initializing the Mock Data (CSV)

To test this, we need to load initial records into the database. Create a file named mediahandling.db-Image.csv inside the db/data/ folder.

ID;Name;Description;ImageUrl
22222222-2222-2222-2222-222222222201;Sunset Boulevard;A beautiful vibrant sunset over the city boulevard.;images/image1.jpg
22222222-2222-2222-2222-222222222202;Mountain Peak;The highest snow-capped peak in the northern mountain range.;images/image2.jpg

 

Step 3: UI Annotations (annotations.cds)

In the UI annotations, we expose the virtual displayImageUrl field in the UI.LineItem (List Report) so the dynamic image renders in the table. We expose the actual image (LargeBinary) field inside the UI.FieldGroup (Object Page) so the user gets a file upload prompt when creating or editing a record.

using MyService as service from '../../srv/catalogService';

annotate service.Image with @(
    UI.FieldGroup #GeneratedGroup : {
        $Type : 'UI.FieldGroupType',
        Data : [
            { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Name', Value : Name },
            { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Description', Value : Description },
            { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Upload Image', Value : image }
        ],
    },
    UI.Facets : [
        {
            $Type : 'UI.ReferenceFacet',
            ID : 'GeneratedFacet1',
            Label : 'General Information',
            Target : '@UI.FieldGroup#GeneratedGroup',
        },
    ],
    UI.LineItem : [
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Name', Value : Name },
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Image', Value : displayImageUrl }, 
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Description', Value : Description }
    ],
    UI.HeaderInfo:{
        $Type : 'UI.HeaderInfoType',
        TypeName : 'Image Record',
        TypeNamePlural : 'Image Records',
        Title : { $Type: 'UI.DataField', Value: ID },
        Description : { $Type: 'UI.DataField', Value: Name },
        ImageUrl : displayImageUrl 
    }
);

 

Step 4: The Backend Interceptor (catalogService.js)

This is where the magic happens. We intercept the after('READ') event. If we detect that Fiori stripped our necessary columns (mediaType and ImageUrl), we immediately execute a lightweight database query to fetch them for the visible rows. Finally, we route the URL accordingly.

const cds = require('@sap/cds');

module.exports = cds.service.impl(async function() {
    // Dynamically grab the actual service base path (e.g., /odata/v4/my)
    const srvPath = this.path;

    this.after('READ', 'Image', async (data, req) => {
        const images = Array.isArray(data) ? data : [data];
        if (images.length === 0) return;

        // If Fiori stripped the columns to save bandwidth, we step in.
        if (images[0].mediaType === undefined && images[0].ImageUrl === undefined) {
            
            // Grab the IDs of the rows currently visible on the screen
            const ids = images.map(img => img.ID);
            
            // Query the database directly to fetch the missing fields
            const dbData = await cds.tx(req).run(
                SELECT.from(req.target)
                      .columns('ID', 'mediaType', 'ImageUrl', 'IsActiveEntity')
                      .where({ ID: { 'in': ids } })
            );

            // Stitch the missing data back into the original payload
            images.forEach(img => {
                const match = dbData.find(d => d.ID === img.ID);
                if (match) {
                    img.mediaType = match.mediaType;
                    img.ImageUrl = match.ImageUrl;
                    img.IsActiveEntity = match.IsActiveEntity;
                }
            });
        }

        // Execute the Traffic Cop logic (guaranteed to have the data)
        images.forEach(image => {
            if (image.mediaType) {
                // Route to the OData media stream for uploaded binaries
                const isActive = image.IsActiveEntity !== false;
                image.displayImageUrl = `${srvPath}/Image(ID=${image.ID},IsActiveEntity=${isActive})/image`;
            } else if (image.ImageUrl) {
                // Route to the static CSV path
                image.displayImageUrl = image.ImageUrl;
            } else {
                // Fallback placeholder
                image.displayImageUrl = 'https://dummyimage.com/200x200/cccccc/000000.png&text=No+Image';
            }
        });
    });
});

 

Output:

 

Note: Deploying to SAP HANA Cloud

When transitioning from a local SQLite setup to SAP HANA Cloud, we may encounter an NPM dependency error when starting the hybrid profile.

If the project is running @SAP/cds version 9, but the latest @cap-js/hana driver expects version 10, the standard npm install will fail with an ERESOLVE conflict. We can safely bypass this strict peer dependency check and link the driver by running the following:

npm install @cap-js/hana --legacy-peer-deps
Once installed, cds watch --profile hybrid command will connect to the HANA instance seamlessly.
 

Conclusion

By shifting the data-retrieval logic into the Node.js backend rather than relying on messy UI workarounds or forced hidden columns, we maintain the strict performance benefits of Fiori Elements V4 while delivering a seamless, dynamic user experience.
